text: I'd like to invite to the podium Dr. Wakabayashi. He's going to give us a talk regarding simultaneous laparoscopic resection of colorectal cancer and synchronous liver metastasis. Dr. Wakabayashi is from the Iowa Medical University School of Medicine. Thank you. Due to the unexpected schedule change of Dr. Wakabayashi, I am going to present this session on the behalf of him.

Here we report simultaneous laparoscopic resection of colorectal cancer and synchronous liver metastasis. Patient was a forty-seven-year-old female. Her chief complaint was anemia. She was diagnosed with ascending colon carcinoma with two liver metastases on segment five. The operation procedure was laparoscopic assisted right hemicolectomy and pure laparoscopic segment V segmentectomy.

The surgeon performs a right hemicolectomy, beginning with the mobilization of the terminal ileum by dissecting the peritoneum. A medial approach is used for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy, visualizing the surgical trunk on the superior mesenteric vein. The iliocolic vein and right colic vein are divided, followed by a functional end-to-end anastomosis through an upper median small incision.
The surgeon begins the cholecystectomy by double-clipping and dividing the cystic duct, ensuring secure closure before proceeding with further dissection.

The surgeon uses intraoperative sonographic examination with Sonazoid contrast to visualize two liver metastases located in segment five, confirming their exact location and relationship to major blood vessels.

The surgeon performs pure laparoscopic segment V subsegmentectomy, beginning with liver parenchymal dissection after mobilizing the right side of the liver. Using a laparoscopic harmonic scalpel, the country line is opened to locate the right anterior glistoneal pedicle, which is double-clipped and divided using a tissue sealing device.

The surgeon continues the parenchymal dissection of the liver using a KUSA device, visualizing additional branches of the glistoneal pedicle in segment five. These branches are clipped and divided using the Enseal tissue sealing device.

The surgeon continues parenchymal dissection along the demarcation line using a harmonic scalpel. A peripheral branch of the middle hepatic vein from segment five is identified and clipped using Enseal before resection. Segment five is resected, containing both metastases with a safe surgical margin.

The operator performs a superficial liver parenchymal dissection using a harmonic scalpel, followed by deeper dissection with KUSA or Enseal. The video shows the dissected area of the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon places the resected segment five of the liver into a sterile bag for retrieval through a five-centimeter median incision, which was made for the anastomosis during the colectomy. This is part of a simultaneous laparoscopic resection procedure addressing colorectal cancer and synchronous liver metastasis.

We have accumulated sixteen patients from two thousand two until two thousand ten. Nine were female. Nine and seven were originated from colon. Liver resection was partial in eight. Segmentectomy two, sectionectomy two, and lobectomy four. Pure laparoscopic procedure was twelve cases. Laparoscopic assisted procedure was four cases. Median operation time was three hundred sixty minutes, median blood loss was one hundred sixty three, and median hospital stay was two weeks. In conclusion, simultaneous laparoscopic resection of colorectal cancer and synchronous liver metastasis can be safely and feasibly performed at our institute. Thank you for your attention.
